text:
The wide chemical diversity of natural antioxidants (AOXs) constitutes a real bottleneck for their separation, detection and quantification in various food and biological matrices. Synergism between several antioxidant species makes the measurement of the global antioxidant capacity more physiological and more relevant for assessing their nutritional benefit. However, there is not yet one single official method acceptable for food labelling purposes due to a lack of a universally accepted quantification procedure.

Presently, evaluation of antioxidants in various biological systems is carried out using numerous tests which may be broadly classified into two classes based on the electron transfer (ET) and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanisms. However, it has been observed that results obtained from different tests vary because of factors such as differences in redox potential, pH optima, solvent effects and mode of action of different antioxidants. The present study therefore attempts to establish a more rational basis for classification of different antioxidant tests that may help in understanding their characteristic limitations and limitations and mutual interactions of different antioxidants. In this context it is believed that the integration of the expertise and experience of the members of the task group by the way of understanding various measurement procedures which have been in vogue in different laboratories will help in making antioxidant work more comparable, meaningful and quantitative.
